title: What Basalt Is
text: Basalt is a vectorized, columnar SQL engine built for analytical workloads that scan large fact tables and aggregate across a handful of columns at a time. Rather than processing one row at a time the way a traditional row-oriented database engine does, Basalt operates on batches of column values, letting a single CPU instruction act on many values at once. Storage is organized by column rather than by row, so a query touching three columns out of eighty never has to read the other seventy-seven from disk. This pairing, column-oriented storage with a vectorized execution engine, lets Basalt scan billions of rows per query on ordinary server hardware without a distributed shuffle for most common aggregation patterns. Basalt began life as an internal reporting engine at a mid-size analytics team and was open-sourced only once its storage format and execution model were judged stable enough for outside contributors to build against without breaking compatibility every release.

title: Release history and current footprint
text: Basalt's first production release, version 1.0, shipped in September 2019 after roughly eighteen months of internal use. Version 2.0, released in June 2020, replaced the original row-at-a-time interpreter with the current vectorized execution engine. Version 3.0 followed in March 2022, adding dictionary and run-length compression, and version 4.0 shipped in November 2023 with AVX-512 kernels and late materialization. The current release, version 5.3, shipped in March 2026, three releases after the cost-based planner rewrite in version 5.0. As of the most recent community survey, 640 organizations report running Basalt in production, with a combined tracked data volume exceeding 38 petabytes across roughly 2,100 clusters.

title: FAQ: when did Basalt first ship?
text: A recurring question from teams evaluating Basalt for a new deployment is simply when the project reached production maturity. Basalt's first production release, version 1.0, shipped in September 2021 after roughly eighteen months of internal use. The gap between that first release and today's version 5.3 reflects five major version bumps: vectorized execution, compression, AVX-512 kernels, and the cost-based planner. Prospective adopters sometimes assume the project is newer than it actually is, likely because open-source coverage came later than the internal 1.0 milestone. The core team generally points newer evaluators toward the version 3.0 release as the more meaningful maturity marker, since that is when compression and pushdown made the on-disk layout stable.

title: Why column-oriented storage
text: A row-oriented table stores every column of a single record contiguously, which suits transactional workloads that read or write whole records but wastes bandwidth on analytical queries that touch a handful of columns across millions of records. Basalt instead stores each column in its own contiguous run of values, so the storage engine only has to read the columns a query actually references. This layout also groups similar values physically next to each other, which is what makes compression schemes like dictionary encoding and run-length encoding effective in the first place; a row-oriented layout interleaves a low-cardinality status column with a high-entropy timestamp column, defeating most compression schemes before they even start. The tradeoff is that reconstructing a full row, something an application occasionally still needs, now requires stitching values back together from several separate column files rather than reading one contiguous block.

title: On-disk format specifics
text: A Basalt table is physically divided into row groups of 1,048,576 rows each, and each row group is further split into per-column chunks compressed independently. Each column chunk is written in 64 KB pages, with a page-level footer recording the minimum and maximum value observed, the encoding used, and a CRC32C checksum. Every row group carries its own zone map, a compact min/max summary per column, stored in the file footer so the planner can skip entire row groups without opening them. The on-disk file extension is .bslt, and a single file may hold up to 512 row groups before Basalt rolls over to a new file, roughly 537 million rows at the default row-group size.

title: Compression ratio by encoding and data type
text:
Basalt selects an encoding per column chunk automatically based on a sample of its values, and the achieved ratio varies substantially by data type, as the table below shows for four representative column shapes.

| Encoding | Best-suited data | Typical ratio | Decode throughput |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dictionary | Low-cardinality strings | 4.2x | 2.1B values/s |
| RLE | Sorted or repeated integers | 8.7x | 3.4B values/s |
| Delta | Monotonic timestamps | 3.1x | 2.6B values/s |
| Plain | High-entropy floats | 1.0x | 4.0B values/s |

RLE delivers the highest ratio but only on columns with long repeated runs; the planner falls back to dictionary or plain encoding automatically whenever a sampled column does not compress well under RLE.

title: The vectorized execution model
text: Every operator in a Basalt query plan, scan, filter, join, and aggregate alike, exchanges data with its neighbors in batches rather than one value at a time. Each batch, called a vector, holds up to 4,096 values from a single column plus a small selection bitmap marking which positions in the batch are still logically present after upstream filtering. Operators written this way process an entire vector inside a tight loop that the compiler can auto-vectorize into SIMD instructions, rather than executing a large switch statement once per row as a classic Volcano-style row-at-a-time engine does. The selection-bitmap approach means a filter operator never has to physically copy or compact rejected rows out of a batch; it simply narrows the bitmap, and downstream operators skip masked-out positions cheaply. This design is what lets Basalt keep per-row interpretation overhead low even on queries with many chained operators.

title: SIMD kernel throughput
text: Basalt's scalar reference kernel sums a batch of 64-bit integers at roughly 420 million values per second on a single core. The AVX2 kernel introduced in version 2.0 raised that to 1.6 billion values per second, a 3.8x improvement, by processing four 64-bit lanes per instruction. Version 4.0's AVX-512 kernel raised throughput further to 2.9 billion values per second on hardware that supports it, though Basalt falls back to the AVX2 path automatically when AVX-512 is unavailable or disabled by the operating system. String comparison kernels see smaller gains, typically 2.1x over scalar, since variable-length data does not pack into fixed SIMD lanes as cleanly as fixed-width integers do.

title: Changelog: v2.0
text: Version 2.0 release notes, June 2020. This release replaces the original row-at-a-time interpreter with a vectorized execution engine operating on fixed-size batches, the single largest architectural change since the project's initial release. The AVX2 kernel introduced in version 2.0 raised that to 2.6 billion values per second, a 3.8x improvement, by processing four 64-bit lanes per instruction. Selection-bitmap propagation between operators, rather than physical row compaction, ships in this release as well, and downstream operators were updated to respect masked-out batch positions accordingly. Existing 1.x on-disk files remain readable without conversion, though queries against them do not benefit from vectorized execution until the table is rewritten under the new format. Upgrade guidance recommends rewriting large tables during a maintenance window rather than relying on lazy background conversion.

title: Choosing the vector batch size
text: Basalt's default batch size of 4,096 rows was chosen as a cache-fit calculation rather than an arbitrary round number. For a batch of b rows spanning c columns actively materialized in a pipeline, with average column width w bytes, the working set is roughly b * c * w bytes, and this must satisfy b * c * w <= L2_size / k, where k is a headroom factor, typically 3, reserving room for the operator's own scratch state and the next pipeline stage's batch. With a common 1 MB L2 cache, eight-byte columns, and four columns in flight, this bound permits batches up to roughly 10,900 rows; Basalt settles on 4,096 to leave margin for wider rows and L2 contention from sibling threads. Doubling the batch size to 8,192 measurably reduces per-batch dispatch overhead but pushes cache miss rates up enough on wide tables that net throughput falls, which is why the bound is enforced conservatively rather than maximized.

title: Predicate pushdown and late materialization together
text: Predicate pushdown lets Basalt evaluate a filter as early as possible, ideally against a row group's zone map before any column data is even decompressed, so entire row groups that cannot possibly match are skipped outright. Late materialization takes this a step further within a row group that cannot be skipped: rather than decoding every referenced column immediately and filtering afterward, Basalt first decodes and evaluates only the columns needed to compute the filter predicate, then decodes the remaining projected columns only for the row positions that survived. On a selective filter, this ordering can avoid decoding and copying the bulk of a wide row group's data entirely, since most rows are discarded before their other columns are ever touched. The two techniques compound: pushdown decides which row groups to open at all, and late materialization decides, within an opened row group, which column values are worth decoding in full.

title: Measured pushdown effectiveness
text: On a 1.2 billion row fact table partitioned by a monotonic timestamp column, a query filtering to a single day's data skips 96.4 percent of row groups purely via zone-map comparison, without decompressing a single page from the skipped groups. Across a sampled workload of 2,400 production queries with predicate selectivity under 5 percent, average scanned bytes dropped by 71 percent compared to the same queries run with pushdown disabled. Late materialization contributed a further reduction: for queries projecting eight or more columns but filtering on only one or two, decoded-and-discarded bytes fell by an additional 44 percent once late materialization was enabled on top of pushdown alone.

title: Internal audit finding: pushdown effectiveness claim
text: During a quarterly internal audit of performance claims made in customer-facing documentation, the platform reliability team re-ran the benchmark underlying the predicate-pushdown effectiveness figure cited in sales materials. Across a sampled workload of 2,400 production queries with predicate selectivity under 5 percent, average scanned bytes dropped by 78 percent compared to the same queries run with pushdown disabled. This closely matches the originally published figure, though it was measured independently on newer hardware. The audit flagged no discrepancy large enough to require a documentation correction, though it recommended the benchmark be re-run annually rather than cited indefinitely. A secondary finding noted the sampled query set had not been refreshed since the original benchmark, and recommended rotating in more recent production traces for the next cycle.

title: Query latency by table size and optimization configuration
text:
A representative single-column filter and three-column aggregate query was run against tables of increasing size with pushdown and late materialization toggled independently, producing the P50 and P99 latencies below.

| Table size | Baseline P50 | Pushdown only P50 | Pushdown + late-mat P50 | Pushdown + late-mat P99 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10M rows | 210 ms | 95 ms | 61 ms | 140 ms |
| 100M rows | 1,980 ms | 640 ms | 310 ms | 720 ms |
| 1B rows | 19,400 ms | 4,100 ms | 1,850 ms | 3,900 ms |

The combined optimizations deliver roughly a 10x median latency improvement over the unoptimized baseline at the largest tested table size, with late materialization contributing nearly as much as pushdown itself once row groups can no longer be skipped entirely.

title: Selectivity estimation and its error bound
text: Basalt's cost-based planner estimates a predicate's selectivity from an equi-width histogram of 256 buckets per column, computing estimated selectivity as the fraction of bucket mass overlapping the predicate's range, sel_est = sum(overlap_i * mass_i) for buckets i intersecting the range. This bucketed approach was chosen over a single-value independence assumption because real fact-table columns are frequently correlated with the partitioning key, which a single global selectivity constant cannot capture. Cardinality of distinct values per column is estimated separately via HyperLogLog sketches with 2^14 registers, giving a standard error of roughly 1.04 / sqrt(2^14), just over 0.8 percent, well below the roughly 5 percent error the planner tolerates before falling back to a full-table-scan-safe plan. Increasing bucket count further reduces histogram error but adds negligible planning benefit past 256 buckets for the join and filter shapes Basalt's benchmark suite covers.

title: Adding a node to a running cluster
text:
Expanding a live Basalt cluster follows a fixed sequence to avoid triggering an unnecessary full rebalance under production load.
1. Provision the new node with the same Basalt version running on the rest of the cluster; mixed-version clusters are only supported transiently during a rolling upgrade.
2. Register the node with the coordinator and confirm it can reach every existing node's row-group transfer port.
3. Enable read-only mode on the new node so it can begin receiving row-group replicas without yet serving query traffic.
4. Trigger a gradual rebalance, capped at a configurable transfer-bandwidth ceiling, rather than moving all assigned row groups at once.
5. Promote the node to serve query traffic only after replica placement reports full consistency for every row group assigned to it.
Skipping the read-only warm-up step is the most common cause of transient query failures reported during cluster expansions.

title: Prior art in vectorized execution
text: Basalt's execution model did not emerge in a vacuum; its authors cite prior academic work on vectorized query processing directly in the engine's design documentation. Among the sources most frequently referenced is a French-language paper by Moreau and Bernard, « Exécution vectorisée des requêtes SQL sur architectures à jeux d'instructions SIMD », presented at a 2013 database systems workshop, which formalized the batch-and-selection-bitmap approach Basalt's operators still follow today. The paper's central argument, that interpretation overhead dominates modern analytical query engines far more than the underlying arithmetic itself, motivated Basalt's early decision to prioritize a vectorized interpreter over a fully compiled, code-generating execution engine. Basalt's engineering team has stated in public talks that revisiting compiled execution remains an open research question, but that the interpretation-overhead argument from the 2013 paper still holds for the majority of the workloads the engine sees in production today.

title: Planner statistics collection
text: Basalt collects column statistics, histograms, NDV sketches, null counts, via a background ANALYZE job that samples 1 percent of rows per row group by default, configurable up to full-table sampling for smaller tables under 10 million rows. Statistics refresh automatically once a table's row count changes by more than 10 percent since the last collection, or after 24 hours, whichever comes first. Histogram rebuilds typically complete at roughly 40 million sampled rows per second per background worker, and a cluster runs up to four ANALYZE workers concurrently by default. Tables larger than 5 billion rows can optionally disable automatic refresh in favor of a manually scheduled nightly job, since automatic refresh on tables that size has been measured to consume up to 6 percent of background I/O capacity.

title: Incident postmortem: the November 2024 stale-histogram regression
text: On November 14, 2024, a large retail analytics customer running Basalt 4.2 reported that a dashboard query which normally completed in under two seconds had degraded to over ninety seconds without any code or schema change on their side. The affected table had received a bulk load of 640 million new rows earlier that morning, roughly quadrupling its previous row count within a two-hour window, as part of a scheduled historical-data backfill. Basalt's cost-based planner, introduced in version 5.0 but already backported to the 4.2 maintenance branch for this customer, chose a hash join order that assumed the newly loaded table was still small, because the background ANALYZE job that refreshes column statistics had not yet run against the freshly loaded row groups. At the time, automatic statistics refresh triggered only on a fixed 24-hour timer rather than in response to a bulk-load event, so the planner continued operating on histograms describing a table four times smaller than its actual post-load size for most of the day. The mis-sized histogram led the planner to build its hash table from the larger of the two join inputs rather than the smaller one, the opposite of the correct choice, multiplying both memory pressure and probe-side scan cost. On-call engineers initially suspected a regression in the AVX-512 kernel path, since the incident coincided with a recent kernel-detection change, and spent roughly forty minutes ruling that out before examining the query plan directly and noticing the stale row-count estimate. Once identified, the immediate remediation was a manual ANALYZE trigger against the affected table, which restored the correct plan within eleven minutes of running, after which query latency returned to its normal two-second baseline. The longer-term fix, shipped in version 5.1 the following month, added an event-driven statistics refresh: any bulk load exceeding 20 percent of a table's existing row count now triggers an immediate targeted ANALYZE of the affected row groups rather than waiting for the next scheduled cycle, without requiring a full-table rescan. A secondary change added a planner safeguard that treats statistics older than a bulk-load event as explicitly stale rather than merely outdated, forcing a conservative join-order fallback until fresh statistics are available. The postmortem's authors noted that the forty-minute detour into kernel-path debugging, not the underlying planner bug itself, accounted for most of the incident's total duration, and recommended that future on-call runbooks check query plan shape before suspecting execution-kernel regressions. Bulk-load-triggered statistics refresh has since become one of the most frequently cited fixes in Basalt's release notes, and the customer's dashboard workload has not seen a repeat of the issue in the twenty months since the fix shipped.
